content: The function $f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}$ has a local minima at $x$ equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2} \\ & \text { Put, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x= \pm 2 \\ & \text { Now, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{6}{x^3} \\ & \text { At } x=2, \quad f^{\prime \prime}(2)=\frac{6}{8}>0 \\ & \text { At } x=-2, \quad f^{\prime \prime}(-2)=-\frac{6}{8}<0 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Thus, } f(x) \text { has local minima at } x=2 \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

content: Given a curve $y=7 x-x^3$ and $x$ increases at the rate of 2 units per second. The rate at which the slope of the curve is changing, when $x=5$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, }\\ &y=7 x-x^3 \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=7-3 x^2$$

Thus, slope, $\quad s=7-3 x^2$

Now, differentiating w.r.t. ' $t$ ', we get

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \frac{d s}{d t} & =-6 x \frac{d x}{d t} \\ \therefore & \left.\frac{d s}{d t}\right|_{\text {at } x=5} & =-6 \times 5 \times 2 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad\left[\because \frac{d x}{d t}=2 \text { units } / \mathrm{s} \text { (given) }\right] \\ & =-60 \mathrm{units} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

To find the derivative of $$x^{2 x}$$ with respect to $$x$$, we use the logarithmic differentiation technique due to the variable exponents. Let's define the function as $$y = x^{2x}$$ and then differentiate both sides with respect to $$x$$.

First, we take the natural logarithm of both sides to simplify differentiation:

$$\ln{y} = \ln{(x^{2x})}$$

Using properties of logarithms, we can simplify the right hand side:

$$\ln{y} = 2x\ln{x}$$

Next, differentiate both sides with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = 2\ln{x} + 2x\cdot\frac{1}{x}$$

Simplifying gives us:

$$\frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = 2\ln{x} + 2$$

Remember that $$y = x^{2x}$$, so substituting back gives us:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = x^{2x}\left(2\ln{x} + 2\right)$$

Therefore, the derivative of $$x^{2x}$$ with respect to $$x$$ is $$2x^{2x}(1 + \log{x})$$.

Thus, the correct answer is Option C: $$2x^{2x}(1+\log{x})$$.

content: The interval in which the function $$f(x)=2 x^3+9 x^2 +12 x-1$$ is decreasing is :
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =2 x^3+9 x^2+12 x-1 \\\\ f^{\prime}(x) & =6 x^2+18 x+12-0 \end{aligned}$$

for decreasing function $$f^{\prime}(x)<0$$

$$\begin{aligned} 6\left(x^2+3 x+2\right) & <0 \\\\ 6(x+2)(x+1) & <0 \end{aligned}$$

$$f(x) \text { is decreasing in interval }(-2,-1)$$

content: The area of the circle is increasing at a uniform rate of $2 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s}$. How fast is the circumference of the circle increasing when the radius $r=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let radius of the circle be } r \mathrm{~cm} \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Given } & \frac{d A}{d t} & =2 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\ & \text { since } & A & =\pi r^2 \\ & \therefore & \frac{d A}{d t} & =2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Also, circumference, } C=2 \pi r\\ &\therefore \quad \frac{d C}{d t}=2 \pi \frac{d r}{d t}\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{llrl} \text { from (i), } & 2 =2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d r}{d t} =\frac{1}{\pi r} \end{array}$$

Now, substituting the value of $\frac{d r}{d t}$ in eq. (ii) we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d c}{d t} & =2 \pi \frac{1}{\pi r}=\frac{2}{r} \\ \text{Now,}\quad\left.\frac{d C}{d t}\right|_{\text {at } r=5} & =\frac{2}{5} \\ & =0.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, circumference of circle increases at the rate of $0.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$.

Given, $x=a \sin ^3 \theta$ and $y=a \cos ^3 \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta \\ & \frac{d y}{d \theta}=-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta \\ & \text { and } \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta}{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =-\cot \theta \\ & \text { and } \quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d \theta}(-\cot \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d x} \\ & =\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta \frac{1}{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =\frac{1}{3 a \sin ^4 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & \text { Now, }\left.\quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right|_{\text {at } x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{3 \cdot a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^4 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3 \cdot a \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3 a} \end{aligned}$$

content:
37. A rectangular visiting card is to contain $24 \mathrm{sq} . \mathrm{cm}$. of printed matter. The margins at the top and bottom of the card are to be 1 cm and the margins on the left and right are to be $1 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~cm}$ as shown below:

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

(i) Write the expression for the area of the visiting card in terms of $x$.

(ii) Obtain the dimensions of the card of minimum area.
explanation:
(i) Given, $x=$ length of printed area

$y=$ breadth of printed area

Area of printed part $=x y$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad 24 & =x y \\ \text { or, }\quad y & =\frac{24}{x}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Now, area of visiting card $=(x+3)(y+2)$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{or, }\quad & A=(x+3)(y+2) \\ \text{or, }\quad & A=(x+3)\left(\frac{24}{x}+2\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad & A=\frac{(x+3)(24+2 x)}{x} \\ \text { or, }\quad & A=\frac{2 x^2+30 x+72}{x} \\ \text { or, }\quad & A=2 x+30+\frac{72}{x} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (ii) We have, Area, } A=2 x+30+\frac{72}{x}\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d A}{d x}=2+0-\frac{72}{x^2} \\ \text { and } & \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}=\frac{144}{x^3} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

for minimum area, put $\frac{d A}{d x}=0$

i.e., $$2-\frac{72}{x^2}=0$$

or, $$x^2=36 \Rightarrow x= \pm 6$$

At $x=6 \cdot \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}>0$ Hence, minimum

Therefore, length of vising card

$$\begin{aligned} & =x+3=6+3=9 \mathrm{~cm} \\ \text { and breadth of visiting card }\quad & =y+2=\frac{24}{x}+2 \\ & =\frac{24}{6}+2=6 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

content:
(a) The median of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of $$2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$$. Find the rate at which its side is increasing.

OR

(b) Sum of two numbers is 5. If the sum of the cubes of these numbers is least, then find the sum of the squares of these numbers.
explanation:
Let the length of the median be x cm.

and side of equilateral triangle be $$y \mathrm{~cm}$$

In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$$

$$\begin{aligned} A B^2 & =A D^2+B D^2\left(\angle D=90^{\circ}\right) \\\\ y^2 & =x^2+\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3}{4} y^2 & =x^2 \\\\ y^2 & =\frac{4}{3} x^2 \\\\ y & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} x \\\\ \frac{d}{d t}(y) & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{d}{d t}(x) \\\\ \frac{d y}{d t} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{d x}{d t} \\\\ \frac{d y}{d t} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times 2 \sqrt{3}\\\\ &=4 ~\text{cm/s} \end{aligned}$$

Hence side of equilateral triangle be increase at $$4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$$

OR

(b) Let the number be $$x$$ and $$y$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad x+y & =5 \quad \text{.... (1)}\\\\ S & =x^3+y^3 \quad \text{.... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

from (1) & (2)

$$\begin{aligned} S & =x^3+(5-x)^3 \\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =3 x^2-3(5-x)^2 \\\\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =3 x^2-75+30 x-3 x^2 \\\\ & =-75+30 x \end{aligned}$$

for maximum and minimum

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d S}{d x} & =0 \\ -75+30 x & =0 \\ x & =\frac{75}{30}=\frac{5}{2} \\\\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =-75+30 x \\\\ \frac{d^2 S}{d x^2} & =30 \\\\ \frac{d^2 S}{d x^2} & >0 \end{aligned}$$

Hence $$S$$ is minimum at $$x=\frac{5}{2}$$

Minimum value of $$x^2+y^2$$

$$=\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}=\frac{25}{2}$$
